title: Operations methods
summary: How a business physically makes its product shapes its cost per unit, its quality, its flexibility and its ability to grow. This lesson works through the whole of syllabus 5.2: the methods of production — job (one-off/bespoke), batch, mass/flow (line) and mass customisation — with their features, advantages and disadvantages; the factors that influence the choice of method (the nature of the product, the size of the market, the resources available and cost); how technology and automation are reshaping operations; and what it takes to change from one method to another. Every worked example is set out exactly the way our marking engine awards Business Management marks — AO1 knowledge, AO2 application to context and AO3 analysis and evaluation — so you can see why a supported judgement, not a memorised list, is what earns the top band.
learningObjectives: Distinguish between job, batch, mass/flow (line) and mass customisation production, and state the features, advantages and disadvantages of each.
Explain the factors that influence the choice of production method — the nature of the product, the size of the market, the resources available and cost.
Analyse how technology and automation are changing the way businesses organise production.
Explain what is involved when a business changes from one production method to another.
Recommend the most appropriate production method for a business by applying the concepts to context and reaching a supported judgement.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: How you make it, not just what you make
simpleExplanation.summary: A production method is the way a business organises the process of turning inputs into a finished good or service. The same idea — a chair, a loaf, a car, a pair of trainers — can be made as a single bespoke item, in small identical groups, or by the million on a continuous line. The method a business picks decides how much each unit costs, how flexible it can be, and how high the quality feels.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think about a kitchen. Cooking one elaborate wedding cake to a couple's exact design is job production — one unique item, skilled hands, high price. Baking a tray of 200 identical sourdough loaves, then cleaning down and switching to croissants, is batch production — groups of identical goods, some variety between runs. A sandwich chain assembling the same wrap over and over along a counter is flow (line) production — continuous, standardised, cheap per unit. And a build-your-own-bowl counter, where a fast standard line still lets each customer pick their own toppings, is mass customisation — flow-style efficiency with job-style choice.
steps: Start with the nature of the product. Is it a unique, complex, high-value item, or a standardised good the market wants in huge volumes?
Weigh the size of the market. A single order points to job; a steady stream of orders for variants points to batch; mass demand for one standard product points to flow.
Check the resources. Job and batch lean on skilled, flexible labour; flow and mass customisation lean on heavy capital investment in machinery, automation and IT.

Bring in cost. Flow gives the lowest cost per unit only at high volume; below that, its huge set-up cost makes batch or job cheaper. Then recommend and justify the method that best fits THIS business.

flashcards.back: Making a single, unique item to a customer's individual specification — a 'one-off'. Labour-intensive and reliant on skilled, flexible workers. High quality and full customisation, but high cost per unit and slow. Example: a bespoke suit, a custom-built yacht.

Making a group (a 'batch') of identical products together, completing each stage for the whole batch before moving on. Machinery is cleaned and reset between batches, allowing variety. Cheaper per unit than job, but downtime and work-in-progress build up between runs. Example: a bakery producing 200 loaves, then switching to bread rolls.

The continuous production of large quantities of identical, standardised products moving along a production line, with a simple task at each stage. Highly capital-intensive and automated. Very low cost per unit from economies of scale, but inflexible with a huge set-up cost. Example: bottling soft drinks, assembling cars.

Using flexible, computer-controlled production systems to make customised products on a large scale — combining the low unit cost of flow production with the variety of job production. Example: configuring your own laptop online, or personalised trainers.

A production process that relies heavily on machinery and equipment relative to labour. Strongly associated with flow production and mass customisation, where automation drives down the cost per unit at high volume.
A production process that relies heavily on human workers relative to machinery. Strongly associated with job and batch production, where skilled, flexible people do varied work.

The fall in average cost per unit as output rises. Flow production captures these most fully by spreading a huge fixed set-up cost over millions of identical units — which is why it is only cheapest per unit at very high volume.

The use of machinery, robotics and computer control to carry out tasks with little direct human labour. Automation lowers unit costs, improves consistency and enables mass customisation, but requires heavy capital investment and can reduce the number of low-skilled jobs.

The time from receiving an order to delivering the finished product. Job production has the longest lead time because each item is made from scratch; flow production has the shortest for a product already on the line.

flashcards.front: Trap: 'flow production is always the cheapest method'
flashcards.back: False. Flow production has enormous fixed set-up costs. It is only the cheapest PER UNIT when producing vast quantities of a standardised product. At low volumes, or for varied products, batch or job production is more cost-effective.

flashcards.front: Trap: 'job production is inefficient and outdated'
flashcards.back: Not so. For high-value, bespoke products it is the only viable method: it delivers maximum flexibility and craftsmanship and can command a premium price, making it highly profitable in luxury markets.

No. Mass customisation lets customers choose from set options on a FLOW system, keeping unit costs low. Job production builds a genuinely one-off item from scratch. Choice from a menu is not the same as a bespoke design.

quickCheck.prompt: A workshop builds one custom-designed racing yacht to a client's exact specification. Which production method is this?
options: Job production, because it is a unique one-off item
Batch production, because yachts are made in groups
Flow production, because it uses skilled labour
Mass customisation, because the client chose the design
quickCheck.answer: Job production, because it is a unique one-off item

quickCheck.prompt: What is the key difference between batch and flow (line) production?
options: Batch makes a group of identical items and resets between runs to allow variety; flow continuously makes one standardised product with no changeover
Batch uses machinery and flow uses only hand labour
Batch is always cheaper per unit than flow at every level of output
Batch produces unique one-off items while flow produces groups
quickCheck.answer: Batch makes a group of identical items and resets between runs to allow variety; flow continuously makes one standardised product with no changeover

quickCheck.prompt: A soft-drinks firm produces millions of identical cans a year and needs the lowest possible cost per can. Which method is most appropriate, and why?
options: Flow production, because high, stable demand for one standard product lets it spread its huge set-up cost and gain economies of scale
Job production, because it gives the highest quality per can
Batch production, because it avoids all set-up costs
Mass customisation, because customers want to design their own cans
quickCheck.answer: Flow production, because high, stable demand for one standard product lets it spread its huge set-up cost and gain economies of scale

quickCheck.prompt: Which statement best describes mass customisation?
options: Making a genuinely unique, built-from-scratch product for each customer
Using flexible, computer-controlled systems to offer customer choice at low unit cost on a large scale
Producing one standardised product with no variety at all
Making small identical batches and resetting between each run
quickCheck.answer: Using flexible, computer-controlled systems to offer customer choice at low unit cost on a large scale

quickCheck.prompt: Which is a genuine ADVANTAGE of flow (line) production?
options: It offers each customer a unique, bespoke product
It has very low set-up costs
A very low cost per unit from economies of scale at high volume
It is highly flexible and easy to switch to a new product
quickCheck.answer: A very low cost per unit from economies of scale at high volume

faq.q: Can service businesses use these production methods?
faq.a: Yes — the concepts apply to services too. A luxury restaurant designing a one-off tasting menu for a wedding is using job production. A fast-food chain assembling identical burgers along a counter is using flow production. A training company running the same standard course for group after group, with a reset between them, is using batch production. And a bank offering a standard product that customers configure online — choosing terms, add-ons and limits — is applying mass customisation. Marks in a service context come from identifying the method correctly and applying it to how that service is actually delivered.

faq.q: Is flow production always cheaper than batch production?
faq.a: Only at high volume. Flow production carries an enormous fixed set-up cost — the production line, the machinery, the automation. That cost is spread across output, so the cost per unit only falls below batch once output is large. Below the break-even volume, batch production is cheaper because it avoids the huge fixed investment. This is exactly why 'which method is cheapest?' can never be answered without knowing the size of the market: cost per unit depends on volume.

faq.q: How is mass customisation different from just offering a few product options?
faq.a: Mass customisation is a genuine production strategy, not a menu. It relies on flexible manufacturing systems and computer control so that a customer's individual choices — colour, features, fit — feed directly into an efficient, largely automated line, giving job-style variety at close to flow-style unit costs. Offering three sizes of the same fixed product is just standardisation with variants; mass customisation lets the customer configure the item while the business keeps the low cost and speed of continuous production.

faq.q: What actually changes when a business switches production method?
faq.a: A great deal — which is why the switch is a strategic decision, not a tweak. Moving from batch to flow, for example, means large capital investment in a production line, retraining or reducing the workforce, redesigning the factory layout, and committing to high, stable demand to justify the fixed cost. It can also change the culture and motivation of workers as varied craft work gives way to repetitive line tasks. A firm should only change method when a lasting shift in demand, technology or cost makes the new method's advantages outweigh the disruption and investment of switching.

faq.q: How do I decide which method a case-study business should use?
faq.a: Weigh four factors against the specific business: the nature of the product (unique and complex, or standardised?), the size of the market (one order, a stream of variants, or mass demand?), the resources available (skilled labour, or capital for automation?), and cost (what volume makes each method cheapest per unit?). There is no single 'best' method — the marks come from applying these factors to the given context and justifying a choice, not from reciting definitions.

sections.content: Every organisation that makes a product or delivers a service has to answer one operational question before it produces anything: HOW will it organise production? The answer — the operations, or production, method — shapes the firm's cost per unit, its quality, its flexibility and its capacity to grow. This lesson covers the whole of syllabus 5.2: the four methods (job, batch, mass/flow and mass customisation) with their features, advantages and disadvantages; the factors that drive the choice between them — the nature of the product, the size of the market, the resources available and cost; how technology and automation are reshaping operations; and what is really involved in changing from one method to another. Because 5.2 questions are frequently evaluation-led, the worked examples are set out the way our marking engine actually awards marks, so you can see why a supported judgement outscores a memorised list every time.
The four methods of production

The methods sit on a spectrum from the completely individual to the completely standardised. At one end, job production makes a single unique item; at the other, flow production makes millions of identical ones. Batch sits in between, and mass customisation is a modern hybrid that tries to capture the best of both ends at once. The trade-off running through all four is the same: greater customisation and flexibility on one side, lower cost per unit and speed on the other.
1. Job production (one-off / bespoke)

Job production is the creation of a single, unique item made to a customer's individual specification — a genuine 'one-off'. It is used for complex, high-value goods and personal services where every order differs. Because each product is different, the process is labour-intensive and depends on highly skilled, flexible workers who can adapt to each new brief. Think of a tailor-made suit, a custom wedding cake, a hand-built yacht or a bespoke piece of software written for one client.

items: **Features:** a single unique item per order; skilled, labour-intensive work; general-purpose tools rather than a dedicated line; the longest lead times.
**Advantages:** highest quality and full customisation to the customer's exact needs; can command a premium price; varied, skilled work raises worker motivation and job satisfaction.
**Disadvantages:** very high cost per unit (no economies of scale); slow; needs expensive skilled labour and can be hard to schedule and manage.

sections.content: 2. Batch production

Batch production makes a limited number of identical products in a group, or 'batch'. All the items in a batch move through each stage of the process together; only when a stage is complete for the whole batch does the batch move on. Between batches the machinery is cleaned and reset, which lets the firm switch to a different product — so batch production offers variety that flow cannot, while gaining efficiencies that job cannot. A bakery making 200 sourdough loaves, then resetting to bake bread rolls, then again for baguettes, is running batch production.

items: **Features:** groups of identical items; changeover and reset between batches; some use of machinery; a build-up of work-in-progress as batches wait between stages.
**Advantages:** lower cost per unit than job production; flexibility to offer a range of products/variants; makes better use of machinery and some economies of scale.
**Disadvantages:** downtime and cost when resetting between batches; work-in-progress inventory ties up cash and space; repetitive tasks can lower motivation compared with job work.

sections.question: A furniture firm can make 60,000 identical stools a year by one of two methods. Under BATCH production, fixed costs are $150,000 and the variable cost is $22 per stool. Under FLOW (line) production, fixed costs are $600,000 and the variable cost is $12 per stool. (a) Calculate the total annual cost of each method at 60,000 units. (b) Using your figures, advise which method the firm should use. [6]
sections.solution:
**(a) Calculations.**

Batch: total cost = fixed cost + (variable cost × units) = $150,000 + ($22 × 60,000) = $150,000 + $1,320,000 = **$1,470,000**.

Flow: total cost = $600,000 + ($12 × 60,000) = $600,000 + $720,000 = **$1,320,000**.

**(b) Advice.** At 60,000 units, flow production is cheaper by $1,470,000 − $1,320,000 = **$150,000 a year**, so on cost alone the firm should choose flow production. Flow carries a far higher fixed set-up cost ($600,000 vs $150,000), but its much lower variable cost ($12 vs $22) more than repays that once output is this high, giving a lower cost per unit ($22.00 for flow vs $24.50 for batch). The advice holds ONLY if demand for the identical stool stays high and stable: because flow production is inflexible, if annual demand fell well below this level — or if the firm needed to vary the design — batch production would become the better choice, since its lower fixed cost makes it cheaper at low volumes.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 6 marks:**

- **AO2 — application (up to 2):** correct method and figures — total cost of each option calculated accurately with working and units shown.
- **AO2 — application (1):** a clear comparison — the $150,000 annual saving (or the lower unit cost) identified from the candidate's own figures.
- **AO3 — analysis/evaluation (up to 3):** a recommendation that goes beyond the number — recognising that the flow cost advantage depends on high, stable volume, and that batch wins at low volume or when variety is needed. The engine rewards the reasoning that ties the numbers back to the size of the market, not just the arithmetic.

sections.content: 3. Mass / flow (line) production

Flow production — also called line or mass production — is the continuous creation of large quantities of identical, standardised products. The product moves along a production line and a simple, specialised task is performed at each stage. The method is highly capital-intensive: it relies on automation and machinery to run continuously, at speed, with a low cost per unit. Bottling soft drinks, assembling cars and packaging breakfast cereal are classic flow operations.

items: **Features:** continuous output of one standardised product; a sequenced production line; heavy automation; low-skilled, repetitive tasks at each stage; the shortest lead time for an existing product.
**Advantages:** the lowest cost per unit from large economies of scale; very high output and speed; consistent quality; low labour cost per unit as tasks are simple.
**Disadvantages:** huge initial investment; very inflexible — hard and costly to change the product; a breakdown at any stage can halt the whole line; repetitive work can demotivate staff.

sections.content: When you recommend a method, never stop at a generic claim like 'flow production is cheap'. Anchor it to the business and the volume. Instead write: 'For a soft-drinks producer selling millions of identical cans, flow production is most appropriate because its high, stable demand lets it spread the large set-up cost across a huge output, minimising the cost per can and protecting margins in a price-competitive market.' The application to the specific business and its market is where the AO2 marks live.
4. Mass customisation

Mass customisation uses flexible, computer-controlled production systems to make customised products on a large scale. It aims to give customers the variety of job production at close to the low unit cost of flow production. The customer typically configures the product from a set of options — colour, features, specification — and that choice feeds directly into an efficient, largely automated line. Configuring a laptop to order and personalising a pair of trainers online are everyday examples: the item feels bespoke to the buyer, yet the maker keeps most of the cost and speed advantages of continuous production.

items: **Features:** customer choice from set options fed into a flexible, computer-controlled line; enabled by automation, modular design and data systems.
**Advantages:** combines variety and personalisation with low unit costs; can charge a premium for customisation while keeping efficiency; strengthens customer loyalty and gathers useful demand data.

**Disadvantages:** requires heavy investment in flexible technology and IT systems; more complex to manage than a single standard line; customisation is limited to preset options, not a genuine one-off design.

sections.content: Factors influencing the choice of production method

There is no method that is 'best' in the abstract — the right choice depends on the business in front of you. Four factors do most of the work, and a strong answer weighs them together rather than naming just one.

items: **Nature of the product:** unique, complex or high-value items point to job; a range of similar products made in runs points to batch; a single standardised product wanted in bulk points to flow. Perishable or highly personalised goods change the picture again.

**Size of the market:** a one-off order suits job; a steady stream of orders for variants suits batch; large, stable, mass-market demand suits flow. Volume is decisive because it determines which method is cheapest per unit.

**Resources available:** job and batch rely on skilled, flexible labour; flow and mass customisation demand large amounts of capital for machinery, automation and IT. A firm without the finance to invest simply cannot choose flow, whatever the market.

**Cost:** flow gives the lowest cost per unit only above a certain volume, because its huge fixed set-up cost must be spread across output; below that volume, batch or job is cheaper. The 'cheapest' method therefore cannot be judged without the expected output.

sections.content: Technology and automation in operations

Technology and automation are steadily blurring the old boundaries between methods. Robotics and computer-numerical-control (CNC) machinery let firms automate tasks that once needed skilled hands, cutting unit costs and improving consistency. Crucially, flexible manufacturing and computer-aided design/manufacture (CAD/CAM) make it far cheaper to switch a line from one product or specification to another — which is precisely what makes mass customisation possible. These gains come at a price: large capital investment, a need for high-skilled technicians to run and maintain the systems, and often fewer low-skilled roles, which raises real workforce and ethical considerations.

items: **Lower unit costs and higher consistency:** automation runs continuously and reduces human error, driving down cost per unit and raising quality reliability.
**Greater flexibility:** CAD/CAM and flexible manufacturing systems cut the time and cost of changeovers, so even batch and customised production become more responsive.
**Enables mass customisation:** computer control lets a customer's individual choices flow through an efficient line — job-style variety at near flow-style cost.
**Trade-offs:** heavy capital investment, dependence on skilled technicians and maintenance, and potential job losses or reskilling needs among lower-skilled workers.

sections.content: Changing from one production method to another

As a business grows or its market shifts, it may need to change production method — most often from job or batch toward flow as demand rises, or toward mass customisation as technology allows. This is a strategic decision, not a quick adjustment. It usually demands significant capital investment in new machinery and factory layout, retraining or restructuring of the workforce, and a firm commitment to the level of demand needed to justify the new method's fixed costs. It can also change the nature of work — varied craft tasks giving way to repetitive line roles — with knock-on effects for motivation and industrial relations. A firm should switch only when a lasting change in demand, cost or technology makes the new method's advantages clearly outweigh the disruption and risk of the transition.

items: **Trigger:** a durable rise in demand, a fall in the cost of automation, or a competitive need for lower prices or more variety.
**Costs of switching:** capital investment in machinery and layout, retraining or redundancy costs, temporary disruption to output and possible resistance from staff.
**Risk:** committing to high fixed costs on the assumption that demand will stay high — a downturn can leave an inflexible flow line running below the volume it needs.
**Judgement:** change method only when the long-term gain outweighs the one-off disruption and the ongoing loss of flexibility.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Claiming 'flow production is always cheapest'** — it is only cheapest PER UNIT at high volume, because its huge fixed set-up cost must be spread across a large output. At low volumes, batch or job is cheaper.

**Confusing batch and flow** — batch makes groups of identical items with a reset between runs (allowing variety); flow makes one standardised product continuously with no changeover. Mixing these up undermines the whole answer.

**Treating mass customisation as job production** — mass customisation offers customer CHOICE FROM SET OPTIONS on an efficient line at low unit cost; job production builds a genuinely one-off item from scratch. They are not the same.
**Dismissing job production as outdated** — for luxury, bespoke and high-value goods it is the only viable method and can be highly profitable at a premium price.
**Ignoring the size of the market** — you cannot judge which method is 'cheapest' or 'best' without the expected volume; the answer changes entirely with demand.
**Listing advantages and disadvantages without applying them** — a bare list earns AO1 only; the marks climb when each point is tied to the specific business in the case.
**Recommending without a supported judgement** — an 'evaluate' or 'recommend' answer that gives both sides but never commits to a justified conclusion cannot reach the top band.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

Business Management 5.2 is assessed against three objectives: AO1 rewards relevant knowledge and understanding, AO2 rewards applying that knowledge to the specific business in the stimulus, and AO3 rewards analysis and a balanced evaluation. In the analytic/points scheme each distinct valid point earns credit, but the higher 'evaluate' and 'recommend' marks are reserved for answers that combine APPLICATION to context with a BALANCED evaluation that ends in a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT. Watch how the marks below attach to applied, two-sided reasoning and a justified conclusion — never to a generic list.

sections.question: Recommend the most appropriate production method for a business that makes handmade luxury furniture. [10]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
A business making handmade luxury furniture is defined by the nature of its product: each piece is high-value, individually designed and prized for craftsmanship. That points strongly toward JOB production, where a single, unique item is made to the customer's exact specification. Job production would let the firm meet bespoke requests — a specific timber, dimension or finish — and deliver the very high quality that justifies a luxury price. Because the work is varied and skilled, it also tends to motivate the craftspeople, which in a business selling craftsmanship is itself a source of competitive advantage. The customer is buying uniqueness, so the method must be able to deliver it.

The main drawback of job production is cost and speed. With no economies of scale, the cost per unit is high and lead times are long: every piece is built from scratch by skilled labour, which limits how many the firm can make. If demand grew, job production could become a bottleneck on the firm's ability to expand.

This is where BATCH production becomes the realistic alternative to weigh. If the business could group its work — making, say, ten identical dining tables of a popular design in a run before switching to a batch of matching chairs — it would cut the cost per unit and raise output while still offering a range of products. Batch would suit the firm if the market were larger and customers were willing to buy from a curated collection rather than commissioning a true one-off. However, batch sacrifices the individual customisation that a luxury buyer is paying for: identical items in a run are, by definition, not bespoke, and 'handmade luxury' loses its meaning if pieces become standardised.

The size of the market is therefore decisive. A luxury furniture maker typically serves a small number of high-paying customers who want exclusivity, not a mass market chasing the lowest price. The firm's competitive edge is craftsmanship and uniqueness, and its resources — skilled artisans rather than an automated line — fit job production. Flow production and heavy automation are clearly inappropriate here: the huge fixed cost could never be spread over such low volumes, and mechanised uniformity would destroy the very quality the brand sells.

**Judgement.** On balance, the business should use JOB production. The nature of the product — bespoke, high-value, craftsmanship-led — and a small, exclusive market together outweigh the cost and speed advantages of batch. Job production's high unit cost is acceptable, and even desirable, because luxury buyers expect to pay a premium for a unique piece; a lower price from batch production would not attract this segment and could dilute the brand. The firm could sensibly use batch production for a limited 'signature collection' of popular designs to lift output and margins, but its core offer — and its identity — should remain job production. Here, the priority is quality and exclusivity over cost per unit, so job production is the appropriate choice.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 10 marks:**

- **AO1 — knowledge (production-method concepts):** the engine credits accurate command of the ideas the question turns on — job production as unique/bespoke and labour-intensive, batch production as groups of identical items, economies of scale, cost per unit and the factors influencing choice. Correct terminology signals AO1.
- **AO2 — application to THIS business:** marks are awarded for tying those concepts to the handmade luxury furniture maker specifically — bespoke customer specifications, craftsmanship as the source of premium price, skilled artisans as the firm's resource, and a small exclusive market rather than a mass one. Generic points that never mention the luxury furniture context do not earn AO2.
- **AO3 — analysis and balanced evaluation:** the engine credits developed two-sided reasoning — job's quality, customisation and motivation weighed against its high cost and slow output, with batch considered as a genuine alternative — and, crucially, a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT that commits to job production and justifies WHY quality and exclusivity outweigh cost for this business.
- **What lifts an answer into the top band:** application plus judgement. The engine rewards weighing job vs batch against the nature of the product, the market and the resources, then committing to a reasoned recommendation. It does NOT reward a longer generic list of every method's pros and cons with no decision.
- **What the engine rejects:** all-advantages or all-disadvantages answers with no balance; a 'judgement' that merely restates both sides without choosing; and correct definitions with no link to the luxury furniture business.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — AO1 for the right production-method concepts, AO2 for applying them to the business in the question, and AO3 for balanced analysis ending in a supported judgement. So do not just list what each method is: name the concept (job, batch, flow, mass customisation, economies of scale), apply it to the specific business and its market, weigh at least two methods against each other, and finish by committing to a recommendation with a reason. The application and the judgement are where the top-band marks are won.
Where this leads

The choice of production method underpins much of the rest of operations management. It feeds directly into how a firm manages costs and capacity, its approach to quality, its stock control and lean production, and its ability to grow and innovate. The habit built here is the one that earns marks across the whole course: identify the concept, apply it to the specific business and its market, weigh the alternatives, then commit to a justified judgement.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 question marked: recommend the most appropriate production method for a business, applying the concepts and reaching a supported judgement
